topic: A person's invention is already patented by someone else
raw_output:
[story 1]

The fluorescent lights of the patent office hummed with a mocking indifference. Elias slammed his worn blueprints onto the polished mahogany desk, the sound echoing sharply in the cavernous room. He ran a hand through his already disheveled hair, his jaw tight enough to crack a tooth. "It's mine! I conceived of this mechanism six months before anyone else filed a provisional!" He leaned forward, his voice dangerously low, every syllable laced with a simmering fury that threatened to boil over. The clerk, a woman with impeccably neat spectacles, merely offered a polite, practiced smile that didn't reach her eyes. "Mr. Vance, we've reviewed the prior art. The core concept, as described in patent 88-B, is clearly covered." Elias pushed himself back from the desk, his chest heaving. He felt a hot flush creeping up his neck. He stared at the official document, the ink seeming to mock his years of tireless work. "Covered? That's a blatant appropriation! They've just stapled my life's work onto their filing!"

[story 2]

Maya paced the length of her cramped workshop, the rhythmic tap of her worn boots against the concrete floor the only sound besides the frantic drumming in her ears. She stopped abruptly, spinning around to face the empty space where her prototype—the kinetic energy converter—sat under a dusty sheet. Her knuckles were white where she gripped the edge of the workbench. "Unbelievable," she muttered, the sound tight and brittle. She snatched up the official notification, the glossy paper feeling like sandpaper under her fingertips. The fine print detailing the existing patent number swam before her eyes. A sharp, involuntary gasp escaped her lips. She crumpled the paper in her fist, the sound of the tearing sound disproportionately loud. "They just… took it. After all the sleepless nights, all the sacrifices," she whispered, her voice shaking with a profound sense of betrayal. She kicked a discarded tool across the floor, the movement sharp and uncontrolled.

[story 3]

Dr. Chen stood by the window, the city lights blurring into streaks of meaningless color. He re-read the legal jargon for the third time, his breath coming in short, shallow bursts. A muscle twitched violently in his temple. He clenched his fists so hard that his nails dug crescent moons into his palms. This wasn't just a setback; it felt like a deliberate slight, a calculated theft of his intellectual property. "They didn't just copy the schematic," he articulated slowly, his tone dangerously level, the control barely masking the tremor in his voice. He turned from the window, his gaze fixed on the door, as if expecting the patent holder to materialize and apologize. "This is an outrage. A complete disregard for originality. I will see to it that every avenue is exhausted. They will regret crossing this line."
